fuzzy_prd: hey team, we need to wrap up the persistent worker integration layer we've been discussing. basically the idea is that instead of spinning up the app on every single request, we keep it alive and reuse it — similar to what we did with that session isolation approach in the login module, but applied to the whole request lifecycle here. the main pain points we keep hearing from ops: the server gets stuck in a broken state after certain errors and keeps serving garbage responses until someone manually restarts it; stale database connections are causing intermittent failures on long-running workers; and context from one user's request is bleeding into the next person's error reports which is a compliance nightmare. we also need the worker to gracefully announce when it's starting and stopping so the load balancer can react properly. there's also a thing where some errors should be tolerated (like known app-level stuff) without triggering a full restart, but truly unexpected errors should force a clean rebuild. the metrics setup needs to happen at boot time, not lazily. and the debug dump tool needs to be wired into the request profiler when running in HTTP mode instead of just spewing to stdout. rough priority: get the recycling logic right first, then lifecycle signals, then the db cleanup middleware, then the rest. let me know if anything is unclear, i'll try to get more specs together soon.

trigger_keywords: how should multiple recycling policies be combined
what happens when you chain several reboot voters together
aggregate policy voting logic when more than one strategy is present
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The aggregate recycling policy uses logical OR: it votes to recycle if and only if at least one member policy votes yes. An empty aggregate (zero members) never votes to recycle. The result is a single boolean decision: should_reboot=true or should_reboot=false.

trigger_keywords: which errors should not trigger a worker restart
how does the allow-list work for tolerated error categories
do child categories of an allowed error type also get tolerated
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The error-driven recycling policy checks whether the observed error's category is on the configured allow-list, and this check is hierarchical: an error whose category is a sub-type (prefix match, e.g. 'app_child' is a child of 'app') of an allowed category is also tolerated and does not trigger a recycle vote. Only errors whose category is not covered by the allow-list (directly or via hierarchy) cause a recycle vote.

trigger_keywords: how do you force a recycle with a reason message
what gets emitted when a forced reboot signal is received
can you clear or reset the recycling state mid-request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A force signal carries a human-readable reason string. When processed, the policy records the reason and votes to recycle; the reason is emitted as 'forced_reboot_reason=<reason>' before the 'should_reboot=true' line. A clear/reset signal wipes all accumulated error and force state, returning the policy to abstain (should_reboot=false), regardless of what was observed before the reset.

trigger_keywords: what lifecycle events does the worker emit
in what order are start and stop events fired
does the worker announce itself even with an empty request queue
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The worker always emits 'event=worker_start' before processing any requests and 'event=worker_stop' after the request loop ends. Even with zero queued requests, both events are emitted in that order. A rebuild/recycle emits 'event=kernel_rebooted' between the request completion and the next request (or before worker_stop if it is the last).

trigger_keywords: what happens when the request handler throws an exception
does the worker continue serving requests after a handler failure
is the original error message exposed to the client
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the handler throws, the worker sends a generic HTTP 500 response to the client, stops the underlying worker, and terminates the request loop without processing further queued requests. In non-debug mode the original exception message is suppressed from the response body. In debug mode the message is included. The worker_stopped state is true and lifecycle events still show worker_start then worker_stop.

trigger_keywords: how is the deferred trailing work handled relative to the response
when does post-response work run compared to sending the reply
does deferred work execute before or after the client receives the response
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The worker sends the response to the client first, and only after the response has been dispatched does it execute any trailing/deferred work the handler registered. The output confirms this ordering: deferred_work_ran=true appears in the summary after responses=1 and first_response_content are reported.

trigger_keywords: how does the REMOTE_ADDR token work in the trusted proxy list
what happens to the special peer address token at request time
how is the immediate connection address substituted into the proxy list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The special token 'REMOTE_ADDR' in the configured trusted-proxy list is resolved at request time to the actual remote address of the incoming connection (the peer address). The resolved list replaces the token with the concrete IP, so the output shows trusted_proxies with the literal remote_addr value substituted in place of REMOTE_ADDR.

trigger_keywords: how are metrics declared at startup
what fields does a metric collector definition support
what is the output format for metric declarations
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: At worker startup, each configured collector is declared with: type (gauge, counter, or histogram), namespace, subsystem, help text, label names (comma-separated), and bucket boundaries (comma-separated, histograms only). Fields not supplied in the definition are output as empty strings. Output per collector: metric=<name>, type=<type>, namespace=, subsystem=, help=, labels=, buckets=. Order of collectors matches the input map order.

trigger_keywords: how does the database connection recycling middleware decide to close a connection
when is the liveness probe skipped entirely
what triggers a full application rebuild due to entity manager state
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The connection recycling middleware only probes connections that are both initialized (present) and currently in a connected state. If either condition is false, no probe is attempted and no close occurs. If the liveness probe fails, the connection is closed. For entity managers: a closed/unusable manager forces a full application rebuild (reboot_forced=true); a lazy proxy manager is skipped (framework handles it); an open healthy manager needs no action.

trigger_keywords: how is per-request error reporting context isolated between requests
does a breadcrumb from request one appear in request two's error event
how is sentry or error reporter scope reset between requests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The error-reporting middleware creates a fresh isolated scope for each request. Breadcrumbs or context added during one request must not appear on error events captured during any subsequent request. The test validates this by checking that the final request's captured error event has exactly 0 breadcrumbs when the second request did not add any, even if the first request did add one.

trigger_keywords: how does the var dumper get redirected in HTTP mode
what changes about the debug dump facility when running as an HTTP worker
is the dump output rerouted to the request profiler
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the worker starts under the HTTP runtime mode, the global debug-dump facility (var dumper) is rerouted to the worker-provided dumper so that dumped values are captured by the request profiler rather than written to process stdout. Output confirms: handler_redirected=true and dumped_value=<the value passed>.

trigger_keywords: how are gRPC services registered during container build
what happens when a service implements multiple gRPC contract interfaces
are non-contract interfaces on a gRPC service registered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During container build, services tagged as gRPC services are scanned and registered once per service-contract interface they implement. A service implementing one contract produces one registration; a service implementing multiple contracts produces one registration per contract. Non-contract interfaces the service also implements are ignored entirely. Output format per registration: 'register service=<id> interface=<contract_alias>' in discovery order.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'reboot_chain', 'on_exception', 'worker_trusted_proxies', 'worker_events', 'worker_handle', 'worker_error', 'worker_reboot'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what we did with that session isolation approach in the login module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The per-request error scope isolation middleware described in Feature 6 (feature6_error_scope_isolation.json), which ensures Sentry/error-reporter breadcrumbs and context do not bleed between consecutive requests handled by the same persistent worker process.

context_pointers.vague_reference: known app-level stuff should be tolerated without triggering a full restart
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The allow-list mechanism in the OnExceptionRecyclingPolicy (feature1_2_1_on_exception_reboot.json): errors whose category matches or is a sub-type/prefix of an entry in the 'allowed' list do not cause a recycle vote, while all other categories do.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the debug dump tool needs to be wired into the request profiler when running in HTTP mode
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7 (feature7_dump_handler_redirect.json): the VarDumper handler redirection that reroutes the global dump facility to the worker-provided dumper when mode='http', so output is captured by the profiler rather than sent to stdout.

context_pointers.vague_reference: metrics setup needs to happen at boot time, not lazily
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 (feature3_1_declare_metrics.json): collector definitions (gauge, counter, histogram) with their namespace, subsystem, help, labels, and buckets are declared to the metrics backend during worker startup, not on first use.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't fully specified in the current ticket. Could you check the relevant test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact input/output contract, or look at the PRD section for that feature? Happy to clarify further once you've had a look.

feature1_1_chain_reboot.json.description: Combine several independent reboot voters into one aggregate decision. The aggregate votes to recycle the worker if and only if at least one member votes yes; an empty set never recycles. Input lists each member's vote (true=wants recycle); output reports the aggregate decision.

feature1_2_1_on_exception_reboot.json.description: A reboot voter that decides per-request whether the worker must recycle based on errors seen while handling the request. A fresh voter abstains. Any unexpected error makes it vote to recycle; an error whose category is on the allow-list (including categories that are sub-types of an allowed category) does not. Input is the allow-list of tolerated error categories plus an ordered list of signals observed during the request; output reports the final decision.

feature1_2_2_force_reboot_and_clear.json.description: The same per-request reboot voter can also be told to recycle explicitly via a force-reboot signal carrying a human-readable reason, and its per-request state can be reset. When forced, the voter both records the reason (emitted as a dedicated field) and votes to recycle. Resetting the voter clears any previously observed error or force signal, returning it to abstain. Input is the ordered list of signals (errors, force-with-reason, reset); output reports any forced reason followed by the final decision.

feature2_1_trusted_proxies.json.description: On startup the worker can be configured with a trusted-proxy list so client IP / forwarded headers are honored only from those proxies. The special token meaning 'the immediate peer address' is resolved at request time to the actual remote address of the incoming connection. Input provides the configured proxy list (which may include that special token), the trusted-header bitmask, and the peer address of an incoming request; output reports the effective resolved proxy list and the active trusted-header set.

feature2_2_lifecycle_events.json.description: The worker emits lifecycle events as it runs: a start event before it begins accepting requests and a stop event once the request loop ends. With no requests queued, the worker still announces start then stop. Input is the number of queued requests (here none); output is the ordered list of lifecycle events emitted.

feature2_3_handle_request.json.description: For each queued request the worker invokes the application handler and sends the produced response to the client. The handler may continue doing deferred work after the response has been sent (the response is dispatched before that trailing work runs). Input is a queued request whose handler yields a body and then performs deferred work; output reports the lifecycle events, the number of responses sent, the first response body, and whether the deferred work executed.

feature2_4_error_stops_worker.json.description: If the application handler throws while processing a request, the worker sends a generic 500 error response to the client, stops the underlying worker, and ends the request loop (it does not proceed to the next queued request). In non-debug mode the original exception message is hidden from the response body; in debug mode the message is included to aid debugging. Input queues two requests against a throwing handler, with a debug flag and the thrown message; output reports the response status, whether the thrown message leaked into the body, whether the worker was stopped, and the lifecycle events.

feature2_5_reboot_after_request.json.description: After each handled request the worker consults its reboot voter. If the voter abstains, the application state is preserved across requests and no rebuild happens. If the voter votes to recycle, the worker rebuilds the application state and emits a kernel-rebooted event before continuing. Input is one queued request plus the per-request reboot votes; output reports how many rebuilds occurred and the ordered lifecycle events.

feature3_1_declare_metrics.json.description: At worker startup, configured metric collector definitions are declared to the metrics backend. Each definition has a type (gauge, counter, or histogram) plus optional namespace, subsystem, help text, label names, and (for histograms) bucket boundaries. The declaration preserves exactly the fields supplied, leaving the rest empty. Input is a map of collector name to its definition; output lists each declared collector with its resolved type, namespace, subsystem, help, labels, and buckets.

cases.expected_output:
metric=gauge
type=gauge
namespace=
subsystem=
help=
labels=hello
buckets=
metric=counter
type=counter
namespace=foo
subsystem=
help=count something
labels=
buckets=
metric=histo
type=histogram
namespace=
subsystem=bar
help=
labels=
buckets=0.1,0.5,1

feature4_grpc_service_registration.json.description: During container build, services tagged as gRPC services are scanned and registered against every service-contract interface they implement. A service implementing one contract registers once; a service implementing several contracts registers once per contract; any non-contract interface it also implements is ignored. Input is the list of tagged services, each with an id and which contract-implementation shape it has; output lists each registration as a (service id, contract alias) pair in discovery order.

cases.expected_output:
register service=simple interface=grpc_single
register service=multiple interface=grpc_foo
register service=multiple interface=grpc_bar

feature5_1_connection_recycling.json.description: Before handling a request, the middleware recycles stale database connections so a long-lived worker never reuses a dead socket. It only inspects connections that have actually been initialized and are currently connected; for those it issues a lightweight liveness probe and closes the connection if the probe fails, leaving healthy connections untouched. Input describes whether the connection service was initialized, whether it reports connected, and whether the liveness probe succeeds; output reports whether a probe was attempted and whether the connection was closed.

feature5_2_manager_reset.json.description: After handling a request, the same middleware inspects each initialized entity manager. A closed (unusable) manager cannot be reopened in-place, so the middleware forces a full application rebuild for the next request. A manager that is a lazy proxy is skipped (the framework reopens it automatically), and a healthy open manager needs no action. Input describes the entity manager's state (closed, lazy proxy, or open); output reports whether an application rebuild was forced.

feature6_error_scope_isolation.json.description: The error-reporting middleware isolates each request's reporting scope so context recorded during one request never leaks into a later one. A breadcrumb added while handling the first request must not appear on the error event captured during the second request. Input is a sequence of requests, each flagged for whether it adds a breadcrumb before capturing an error; output reports the number of breadcrumbs attached to the error event captured by the final request.

feature7_dump_handler_redirect.json.description: When the worker starts under the HTTP runtime, the global debug-dump facility is rerouted to the worker-provided dumper so dumped values are captured by the request profiler instead of being written to the process output. Input is the runtime mode and a value to dump; output reports whether the dump facility was rerouted and the captured value.
